body_txt: Love isn't always on time! Hold the Line is a type of Timed Mission , where instead of trying to complete an objective before time runs out, you have to prevent the enemy from completing their objective until time runs out. Usually their objective is "seize control of the area", so you are defending your base (or a certain character) until reinforcements arrive, the foe gives up, or vital repairs are carried out. Your Defense Need Not Protect You Forever . Sometimes the enemy is an overwhelming force, so actually defeating them, or even surviving much beyond the time limit, would be impossible. Fortunately for you, victory will usually occur instantly and automatically as soon as the time runs out , even if you were moments away from defeat. This is commonly used in Real-Time Strategy games, where success requires a balance between creating defensive structures and units to protect one's objective, and scouting out and eliminating enemy bases to reduce their number. It also crops up in First-Person Shooter games, where you often get to use mounted turrets sporting limitless ammo but poor heat management . Sometimes it might be linked to an Escort Mission , where the player has to escort someone to a location, and then protect them while they, for example, hack into the enemy's computer system. The midway between the Hold The Line and Escort Mission is Protection Mission . This mode of gameplay is popular for multiplayer games, usually with the teams switching after the attackers win or the timer runs out. In many cases, there are several lines, and the criteria for winning (if neither team manages to break through them all) are which team got further or which team got to the same line first. Strategy games of all kinds love this trope — there are very few that do not have an example of a Hold The Line mission. Fictionally, it is often a form of You Shall Not Pass! or Last Stand , but the goals differ. In the first, the objective is to save your friends. In the second, the goal is to defeat as many of the enemy as possible before being killed. In this trope, the objective is to stall for time. Tower Defense has this trope as its central gameplay element. It sometimes leads to a Victory by Endurance . Compare The Siege and Multi-Mook Melee . It is similar to and yet quite different from Holding the Floor ; talking for a long period of time without pause. This is a common means to split the Player Party back up after a Full-Party Sequence , leaving some behind to do this while the usual "active" party continues on to achieve the primary objective. Not to be confused with waiting for someone else on the phone.
